speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number two. My name is Joe [? Barbaro. ?] The survivor is Marion Gottesman. We're doing this interview in Rego Park, New York. And today is June 26, 1996. We were talking about moving from--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yep.

speaker: interviewee
text: --one end of town to the other end.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall the name of the street where you moved to?

speaker: interviewee
text: From [INAUDIBLE] Strasse, we went to Lange Strasse at [INAUDIBLE]. And we were-- and we were few years living there. And then this-- we went to my grandmother's house. We had to live with my grandmother together.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had to move out again?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had to move out again because there was not only laws, even the people were afraid to have Jews as tenants. That's when we had to [BACKROUND NOISE]  move again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. Where did you move to, your grandmother's house?

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was she living?

speaker: interviewee
text: She lived in Lange Strasse at 81 at the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that close by to the--

speaker: interviewee
text: The Lange Strasse is Long Street. It's, you know, translation would be Long Street, went from one end of the town to the other end. So my grandmother's house was in the center, that way.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did your friends move also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes because right across the street from my grandmother was the temple, was the synagogue. And there was an apartment in there. And that's where the family Katz moved to, this-- the boy, Gunter, I talked about and his family.

speaker: interviewer
text: They lived in the temple?

speaker: interviewee
text: They lived-- it was a temple. And in front of the temple was like a little house. I guess it used to be for a-- a caretaker or something like that. And they moved in there.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how you felt being forced to leave one place--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really

speaker: interviewer
text: --then the other place?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember things like that. I guess now it was a fact we had to leave. And I was very happy to move into my grandmother's house to be with my grandmother all the time. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it didn't upset you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. That-- that-- I don't remember that. I don't think I was upset about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your parents, do you remember how they felt?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my parents felt different about it, naturally. But on the other hand, my grandmother went on in years already. So they were happy to be with my grandmother together and could take care of her also.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were allowed to continue living with your grandmother for a while?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. No. Till the end, actually, until we were arrested. But the other thing is that the other few Jews who were still there and had homes, they had to give up their homes. And there were two Jewish houses, which one was-- one was Silverberg house, which was my grandmother's. And the other one was right next door, which was the Haineman house, the name of Haineman. And everybody had to move together in the two houses. Actually, they were one family all the same, not two family house, but big one family houses. And we had in my grandmother's house was my grandmother, us, and three other families. We all had to move together. So it turned out that some people had a kitchen together. Try to have little room as a living room private and a bedroom. And those houses were quite big. Not the rooms were not that big, but the houses were big. So in the one family house what my grandmother have, we ended up living there was three, four families.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the names of the other families?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was a mother with her daughter, Moshaim, and a family, Groontman, and then from the Groontman and Moshaim-- they were somehow related-- the old grandfather, Mr. Moshaim, he also lived there. So actually, it was like four different parties, three different parties and us and my grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that affect you at all? Did that bother you?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't bother me. I was happy that they were there because the family Groontman happened to have a lot, a lot of books, like a little library. And I enjoyed that because all of a sudden, I had all the books I wanted to read [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old were you about at this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] I would say I was-- '38-- I was 11. I must have been about 11, 12 years old, 11.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did your parents feel about other people living in your grandmother's house?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they-- in one way, they were glad that we didn't have to move again. And the other way, it-- it was a way of living. We had to. Personally, how they personally felt about it, I really don't know. I really.

speaker: interviewer
text: We didn't talk too much about your father.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did he do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: I believe you called it a tanner here, people who make from skin, leather. He was in that type of business.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he own his own business?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not that I remember him owning the business. But my grandparents had that type of business also. But not anymore at the time, you know, during the war-- before the war, or right-- right after, you know, '33 when Hitler came. So my father was getting skins and would bring it to the tanners. And they made leather out of it. That's how I remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would he go out to try to sell the leather then, also?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- no. No. No, no, no. That wasn't-- as a matter of fact, I think that was a-- a Jewish businessman-- Jewish business in leather. And he would sell the skins to them. But at one time, he had-- but I don't remember he had his own business. But I don't remember that. He also was a very sick man because he was wounded in 1914, '18 during the First World War. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: He was in the military?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was in the military, yes, like all the German Jews were. So was he [? is. ?] And he had the, I believe, a gas poisoning of some kind, you know, during the war in France or something. And he had the open TB from that. The lungs were affected with it. So he was a very sick man.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did things start getting worse? What are your early remembrances after moving [SIGHS] to your grandmother's house and other people living together with you? Were there restrictions? You mentioned--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was a lot--

speaker: interviewer
text: --you were not allowed to go swimming.

speaker: interviewee
text: There were a lot of restrictions. There were no children left at that particular time, except that one girl, Moshaim. And they-- I don't know for what reason or how come, they moved to Hanover. The others stayed in our house. But they went to Hanover. And so I was the only child. And in that particular time, nobody could leave the town. And then they started the school, the Jewish school in [? Herford, ?] and I had to go to the Jewish school. But also, at the same time, we got the Jewish star already to wear. So nobody could get out of the town but me. I had a pass to go on the train or on the bus to go to [? Herford ?] to school. So with that, I got acquainted with other people in [? Herford, ?] and also, with some Christian people there. And I brought food home and things like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned you had to wear a Jewish star.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe what it looked like?

speaker: interviewee
text: A yellow star, Jewish, like a Magen David, like a Jewish star. And it said, Jude, yellow and black.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did you have to wear it?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the left side of your jackets or outer garments.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel wearing it? Do you remember your feelings about it?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Yes, that I do remember, because in the train where I went, at that time, at one particular time already, they had French prisoners in that area. And the French prisoner of wars [BACKROUND NOISE] also was transported on those trains to go to work. So they saw me, and they tried to talk to me and gave me candy. I still was a child, don't forget. And you felt different. You felt different. I have to say that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you proud to wear the star? Were you ashamed to wear it? What-- do you have any--

speaker: interviewee
text: I never was ashamed to wear it. I was afraid to wear it at times. But I never was ashamed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it cause you any problems by wearing it? Did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, it did cause me problems, because at one time, I had-- I don't know if you're familiar with it. When you had relatives in the States or anywhere out of Germany and you wanted to write to them, you got a little slip at the post office, which you bought, and send it along in your letter. And they could get American stamps for it, for instance, right? So I was the only one who could get out of town. I used to go to the post office in [? Herford ?] and got those little things. I had to buy them for my parents. And I remember very well one time, I was a-- two men came over to me with their badges out. And they were Gestapo then already.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the-- what out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Gestapo, you know, like little--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: --passes that they were from the Secret Service.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: German Secret Service. What-- I don't know what you would call, Gestapo, right? And I had to open up my books and everything, what I had with me for going to school, because they were thinking that I had things in there. And so I had--

speaker: interviewer
text: They were searching you, in other--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah they were searching me then already. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That must have been frightening, wasn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very frightening, very frightening. Very frightening. [? So ?] this was already before they arrested us. So I had--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, we're still talking about the period--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --before you were arrested.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any other memories of-- [SIGHS] how about kindness? Did anyone show you kindness, any non-Jews that were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some friends my father had, because he was born in the town, also, so he grew up with all these people. Even if they didn't show to you outwardly, but they would come at night and brought like food and stuff and would hide it on the side of our building, you know? So that type of kindness we came across occasionally. [? That's ?] [? it. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you-- were there food shortages during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, then already because they had the food stamps, like, you know, to get food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Ration cards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not stamp, ration cards, right? And Jews, of course, got less than the others, much less. So we were lucky that my father had some friends in the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do remember being hungry?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you were arrested.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before? No, no, not really, not really. I [? would ?] [? lie. ?] No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was your father still allowed to work during this time?

speaker: interviewee
text: He didn't work for quite a lot of years. And then there came a law out that-- I don't remember anymore exactly when it-- it was '37. Everything was around '36, '37 that every German factory had to employ some Jews, because they wanted the Jewish business people to get out of business, and that they had to work in other factories, et cetera, in German places.

speaker: interviewee
text: And that time, my father started working also in [? Herford ?] where I went to school. But this was before, actually, I went to school there. And was it '37, '38. And what happened is-- what was the question again? You-- you asked me about my father. If your father was still allowed to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --work in his occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, he worked there, but on a very small salary. I remember that. So, you know, this was also already that he had to work there. And Gunter's father worked in another factory. And I don't know what he did. I forgot already, really. And then they were-- oh, there was one, the Moshaims, the ones who live then in our house, they had a paper factory, a huge big paper factory [BACKROUND NOISE] there. So a lot of people were employed there. But they had to-- they took away the factory then already. So they were very mixed-up times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they pay him for taking the factory?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I have no idea. I really don't know. I know that the factory was [? there ?] in 1938 till the Kristallnacht. So there's so many different things. I don't know if they did get paid, or if they didn't get paid, or whatever happened. And none of that family came back either, so they're the ones who went to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: This Jewish school that you went to--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you enjoy that, that school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in a way I did, because I was with other children together again. That was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have a name?

speaker: interviewee
text: --the main part. Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did it have a name? What was the name of the Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No name, nothing. It was one classroom, and we were about, maybe-- I don't know. There are pictures. There we weren't even 10 children, maybe 10, 12 children, at the most. And each one had a different age group. But we had somewhere to go, and somewhere to be, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: And the teacher was kind to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, very nice. He was the cantor of the synagogue, you know? He was also a teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Levin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Levin.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mr. Levin. I don't know his first name.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he taught you all the subjects?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, he-- he tried to teach each child, actually, individual, because we all had different knowledges from school because we were different ages. But he made the best out of. We learned something, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Even mathematics?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not to me. That's the worst for me still today. I don't even know if we had mathematics at the time. You know, it wasn't a school where there was a real curriculum or anything like that that you had.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were your parents worried about you leaving, going to a different town by train, and--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They were very worried about it. And on the other hand, they were happy because I was the only one who could-- could get out of town. So when they had any kind of connections, I was the-- the little middleman in between already. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did this continue, going to the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: To school? They closed it up when the transport started to [? Russia ?] and to [? Rigga, ?] et cetera. So it must have been in '41, to '41. '40, to '40, because from '40 to '41, I worked already. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Are there any other memories that you'd like to talk about or any thoughts?

speaker: interviewee
text: From that particular time?

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you were arrested still.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know what to say. [LAUGHS] In one way, how I feel about it is I was still with my mother, my father, my grandmother. We were still a family. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you feel scared yourself? Were you nervous?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I ever was scared then as a child, really. I don't have a good memory of feeling of being scared. That came much later. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: But at this time, you felt--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: --safe, more or less? You didn't think--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't feel safe. But it was a way of not-- not knowing better. I didn't know really a normal, normal time as a child. There was always something.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family ever talk about leaving Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We wanted to leave in the worst way. But we could not leave because my father was sick. They didn't let anybody out with open TB, number one. And I had an uncle living in St. Louis, who was the son of my grandmother we lived with there, who came in 1898 to America. I think his uncle came and took him along. He never came back to Europe. And I remember my grandmother always saying, [INAUDIBLE], her son, would never let her, you know, let down. She can go move to America. But he never gave us the-- the visa to go. You know, they-- you needed a certain amount of money from the States, and et cetera. We couldn't leave. Not everybody was the well enough or rich enough [BACKROUND NOISE] to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she request his assistance to provide the necessary--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but--

speaker: interviewer
text: --affidavit and documents?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. And I let him know after I came back. When I came here to America, he came to New York to see me. And I told him, I said, I couldn't understand that he couldn't do anything for us. But he should have tried. Not that my grandmother would have been alive at that time. She was old already. But she didn't have to go through that. You have a son in the States, you could have tried in the worst way, in the early '30s already to get out. That didn't happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did he say, why he didn't try?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was, I believe, in St. Louis a baker. Now, I don't know if he had his own bakery or he worked for somebody or whatever. And all right, there was a depression and everything. But there were Jewish organizations then already. He could have at least tried.

speaker: interviewer
text: You don't feel--

speaker: interviewee
text: And he didn't

speaker: interviewer
text: --he did try at all?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. No, not at all. And then I really found out, also, from my cousin, who I met here again, who is the son of my father's sister who came here in 1933-- no, in 1936, '36 yes, in 1936, '37, around that time. And he was about 15 years old at [BACKROUND NOISE] the time. Now, he came with the children transport. And then was in an orphanage here, I believe, in New York for a few weeks or something like that. And then when it was known that there is an uncle and family in St. Louis, they got in touch with him, and he went to St. Louis and stayed with them. But what I found out that my uncle got paid for him from a Jewish organization to have a foster child. And there were-- then my cousin also told me that he took in other children to get paid for it. So I don't know the circumstances, how well off they were or not. But he could have tried to get my grandmother out. That's the way I feel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me again the name of this uncle. What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Benno Silverberg, Benno, B-E-N-N-O, Benno Silverberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your cousin who lived with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Guy Stern, and he became a very famous man. He's a professor in [INAUDIBLE] university, and we are very close.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anything else before we come to the period that you were arrested that you want to mention?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not that I can really think of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Let's talk about--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --the arrest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened is that we were the last Jews left in Vlotho, because the other transports went already, and they took most of the people. And it was actually my mother, my father, my grandmother, and the family Haineman, which I mentioned before, which were two people over in their 80s. My grandmother was in her 80s. They'd all-- Mr. Moshaim, I was talking about, he was in his 80s. And my mother, and father, and-- and me, we were the young ones. So that was the last transport out of Vlotho. And they came with trucks, and SS, and police, et cetera, and put us on the wagons, on the trucks, and took us to Bielefeld. And in Bielefeld was the next big town to us, they had the-- what was called the [GERMAN], you know, like where they collect-- a collection of people center. And then they took us to Theresienstadt. And that was-- I looked up in the papers. I thought it was July 1, '42. It was August 1, '42.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you arrived in Theresienstadt?

speaker: interviewee
text: That we arrived in Theresienstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know where you were going?

speaker: interviewee
text: A-- a friend of my father's came from Czechoslovakia for one reason or the other in Czechoslovakia, but he was not Jewish, a non-Jew. Any he came-- called my-- had left a note for my father-- we didn't even have a telephone at the time or anything-- to come to his office. He was a dentist in a different town, in [INAUDIBLE] So my father got himself like a little pass that he could go to [INAUDIBLE] for his teeth. That's the way to get out. And he told him that in Czechoslovakia, that they emptied out one big town, and it's a ghetto for Jews in the eastern Czechoslovakian area, and also, for a lot of old people from Germany. And he even told my father at that time, if there would be a way of getting me out to try to get me out of Germany. But it was too late.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember this friend's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, Dr. Venkel, Kert Venkel, I believe. Dr. Kert Venkel, V-E-N-K-E-L, Dr. Venkel.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he tried to warn your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: He warned my father, yes. And we saw him again after the war, my mother and I. When we came back, we naturally went to him to see him.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they come for you? You mentioned a truck.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, they came in with trucks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they physically force you out of the house with their-- how did it happen?

speaker: interviewee
text: We know we had-- there was-- physically, of course, physically because we had to get on the trucks. They told us like a day before to get a certain amount of things we could take along, like clothing, et cetera. And my grandmother was 81 years old at the time. And I never forget the first-- the last thing I-- she said when she stepped out of the house. She said, this is the house where I bore all my children, and now they let me got it. I have to leave. You know, just the-- a matter of fact, what I found out about three, four years ago is that that particular house, the Silverberg house was already in the 1600s registered under the name Silverberg. So the great-great-grandparents must have had it already. So it's like forced out of everything. That's it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what you took with you? Did you have to decide a choice?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, choice of-- I don't know-- two dresses, and two sweaters, and two skirts, or pants. I don't remember. I really don't exactly know what we took along. We didn't have most of the things we didn't get anyway. So it didn't matter what you took along, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So you remember going on this truck?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went on that truck. They took us to Bielefeld. And from all the towns around there was like a collection camp there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay in this camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe two or three days.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not more than that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have food to eat there?

speaker: interviewee
text: They gave us something to eat. That I remember, soup or bread, whatever. It started then already. We weren't surprised at anything at then already. Didn't know what it was ahead of us, but we are surprised, it wasn't anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: You weren't expecting anything wonderful?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, not at that point.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your friend, you mentioned this boyfriend.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he-- he with you in this?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he was in Holland already. He was sent in after the Kristallnacht. They had family living in Holland, so they, the whole family went to Holland. And I saw him the first time again in Theresienstadt. He came in from Holland. There was a camp Westerbork. He was [INAUDIBLE]. And he was in Westerbork in Holland. And then from Westerbork, he came to Theresienstadt. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about Kristallnacht?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- I-- [SIGHS] the funny part is everybody says it was on the 9th of November. I remember it on the 10th of November. So it must have been the two days, the 9th and the 10th. We were in school. And there was one of those gym lessons or whatever had sessions there, and we had to leave. So we went home. We were on the way home. And the closer we came to our house, we saw a crowd of people already in front of their house, in front of my house, our house. And my mother and my grandmother were standing outside. And that was the time when my father was working in [? Herford. ?] And the people who, the old people who still lived in their house there, they-- they-- they were outside. And all what we heard was screaming and the [? SR, ?] you know, in the ghetto uniforms [? was ?] outside. And they broke everything in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned ghetto uniforms. Who was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not, ghetto. I said [? SR ?] uniform. SA, there was the yellow uniforms they had. Not the SS, had the black uniforms and the gray ones. This was the [? SR, ?] I think, was the storm troopers. That's them.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were German, German soldiers?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans, right. So they destroyed the whole--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were they doing? They-- they were--

speaker: interviewee
text: Destroyed the whole house on the inside completely, the furniture, [INAUDIBLE], everything what we had was destroyed. I mean, from then on, we really lived in broken-up pieces already till they took us.

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is when you were still going to your public school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. That was the last day of school, the German school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did anything happen to the synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They broke-- they didn't put it in fire. They broke it up. I think I have pictures in the album there I can show you. Yeah. The few stores, which they--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the hurt people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon me?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they hurt people?

speaker: interviewee
text: They arrested-- they arrested all the men, mostly all the men. Some were sent to Buchenwald and Auschwitz-- Buchenwald and Dachau at the time. A few came back. A few did not come back already. And a younger man, I remember, his name was [? Antz ?] [? Swatchauer. ?] He was married to one of the Groontman girls in our house. And he-- they arrested him, and he went to-- to-- he went to Dachau. And they were notified that he died there. Or he got killed in Dachau then already in '38. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's [SIGHS] another story.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to take a break now.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: And we'll begin tape number three in just a few moments.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
